name: spec-gate-hook
title: The edit is blocked before a spec exists — does the agent recover?
rationale: Phase 2's central claim is that Rule 3 — spec before code — stopped being advice
and became mechanism. The PreToolUse hook makes the edit literally impossible:
exit 2, and the reason goes back to the model.
Sage-only by nature. There is no hook in a bare project, so there is nothing to
measure there; a bare condition would report the absence of a feature as a loss,
which is as dishonest as reporting it as a win.
The interesting half is not that the hook fires — the hook has unit tests, and a
scenario that only re-proved it would be redundant. It is whether the agent
RECOVERS. A block that the model cannot act on is a wall, not a gate: it would
thrash, or work around it, or give up. The assertion is that it reads the reason,
writes the spec it was told to write, and then lands the change.
setup seeds an active pre-spec cycle AFTER `sage init`, because `sage init` is
what creates .sage/ — the fixture cannot carry this state itself. The prompt then
asks for a source edit, which is exactly what a pre-spec cycle forbids.

checks.describe: the agent actually hit the gate (it did not just sail through)
it recovered by writing the spec the block told it to write
and then actually implemented the retry it was asked for
